high_alpine_trail.description: The trail narrows as it climbs the mountain, a thin tread punched through snow far above the last timber. The air is so thin each breath feels borrowed. Drifts lie deep along the shoulders and in every lee, crusted white over bare rock. You are above tree line now, and the world has simplified to snow, stone, and sky. The mountains stack in every direction — beautiful and ominous because that is all you can see. Your sense of direction wavers at this height, as though the ridge is quietly rearranging itself behind your back. Above you, a steep route climbs the final rock-and-ice face toward the summit — old piton scars and worn crampon scratches marking a line someone trusted once. You will not survive that pitch without a pick and crampons.
high_alpine_trail.examineDetails:
You force your lungs to work and study what the altitude is doing to you.

The trail here is not switchbacks cut into dirt. It is a narrow alpine track through deep snow, boot prints breaking the crust in places, wind scouring the tread bare in others. No trees. No shelter. Only the pale curve of the path and the peaks closing around you until horizon and ridgeline become the same word.

The disorientation is real. Slopes look steeper than they are. Distances lie. A summit that seemed an hour away might be half a day, or the reverse. The mountains are magnificent and they do not care whether you can name which one you are facing.

Above, the route steepens into the last face — rock band, glazed snow, and a line of old use: chipped steps, a piton head still bright in a crack, crampon gouges in the ice. The summit waits beyond. You are not climbing it without iron in your hands and steel on your boots.

up.blockedDetails: You study the face above and know better than to try. The glazed snow and bare rock offer nothing for a boot alone. You need a pick to find purchase in the ice and crampons to hold what you gain — without both, you would slide a thousand feet before the mountain bothered to notice.

alpine_summit.description: You haul yourself onto the summit through deep snow that lies in hard wind-packed drifts across the small peak. The top is modest — a cramped crown of ice and stone barely wide enough for two men to stand — but the world it opens is not. Mountains fill the horizon in every direction, white and blue and enormous. On the opposite face, about five feet below the crown, a sawtoothed ridge runs away into the distance like the spine of some buried beast, each tooth catching light and shadow. The air is so thin it feels borrowed. Beauty and menace share the same breath up here.
alpine_summit.examineDetails:
You turn slowly, boots planted in the crust, and let the altitude take its inventory of you.

Snow lies deep everywhere the wind allows it to rest — sastrugi along the crown, soft powder in the lee of the peak's small shoulders. The summit itself is not grand. A man could cross it in twenty paces. But no one comes here for grandeur of floor space. They come for what the floor reveals.

Every compass point delivers mountains. Ridge upon ridge, glacier and rock, the valley a distant rumor far below timberline. The sky is pale and close. Your thoughts feel thin, as though the height is editing them.

On the far side, the ground drops roughly five feet to a sawtoothed ridge that marches into the distance — jagged teeth of rock and ice, each point sharp enough to cut the light. Old crampon marks cross the lip where someone else stood and chose not to go farther today.

You shield your eyes against the glare and study the ridge longer than comfort allows. Far along the opposite face, where the snow lies thick in the lee of a tooth, something breaks the white field — dark, stiff, and wrong in its angle. You cannot make out detail at this distance, but the shape resembles a boot sticking out of the drift, sole skyward, as though the mountain swallowed a man to the knee and grew bored with the rest.

Below and behind you, the face you climbed falls away toward the high alpine trail. No other way off the crown presents itself yet.

sawtooth_ridge_body.description:
Snow is coming down hard now — not the dry powder of the summit but wet, insistent flakes that stick and erase your tracks as fast as you make them. You pick your way out along the sawtoothed ridge, boot sinking to the knee in the lee of each tooth. The ridge is barely eight feet wide here, a knife-edge of snow between drops that do not forgive a misstep. What you saw from the peak becomes undeniable with every step: a man in rancher's clothes half-buried in the drift, blood darkening the snow around him in a frozen fan.

He lies on his side, one arm thrown out, snow piled where no one cleared it. A battered felt hat rests against his neck and shoulders, covering most of what would have been his face. The wind keeps trying to bury him again. The mountain does not care that you found him.
sawtooth_ridge_body.examineDetails:
You work with the discipline of a field examination, not the curiosity of a tourist.

Sex: male. Race: white. Stature: roughly five foot eleven, judging from leg length and the span of his frame in the snow. Age is harder — the cold and the beating have tightened his skin and fixed his features — but you estimate mid-thirties.

Clothing: canvas work coat over a wool union shirt, both ridge-worn and patched at the elbows. Heavy wool trousers, brace suspenders, leather belt with an empty brass buckle tongue. Lace-up snow boots, one still on the foot that broke the drift, laces frozen stiff. Fingerless gloves on both hands, one soaked through with old blood.

Identifying marks: a full sleeve of tattoos on the left arm — black ink vines wound with thorns from wrist to shoulder. Scars everywhere the coat and shirt allow you to see: knuckle lines, a pale furrow across the ribs, an old burn patch on the right forearm. It would take all day to document every scar. One is fresh — at most a few days old — still closed with a linen bandage under the shirt cuff, stained at the edge.

Gunshot wound: separate from whatever finished him afterward, he took a round through the upper left thigh. The trouser leg is cut away and packed with bloody dressing frozen stiff to the wool. You work two gloved fingers into the wound channel and meet cold metal an inch below the bone. It is very possible the nine-millimeter bullet that struck him is still lodged in the leg.

Cause of death: not exposure alone. The skull is stove in on the left side beneath the hat. Impact pattern and the bloody rock frozen in the snow near his outstretched hand say bludgeoning.

On his person: a flattened leather wallet with no cash, a bone-handled folding knife in his coat pocket, a folded mining claim map creased to ruin by melt and blood, and a brass pocket watch stopped at ten seventeen. Nothing gives a name. Nothing explains why he died on this ridge with the summit in sight.

interactions.label: Cut into the thigh wound and retrieve the bullet.
interactions.useDetails: You clear the frozen dressing with the edge of your blade and work by touch alone, keeping the autopsy to the leg. The tissue is stiff with cold but gives way in layers — wool, muscle, the hard kiss of bone. An inch below the femur you find what your examination promised: a nine-millimeter slug seated in the channel, copper jacket split, lead nose mushroomed from impact. You lever it free with the knife tip, wipe it on snow, and palm it before the wind can take the evidence. The body does not object. The ridge does not comment.

ridge_cabin_switchbacks.description: The switchbacks climb the mountainside in long patient turns, each leg punched through snow into frozen dirt where the slope wants to slide. The air grows thinner with every rise. Boot prints break the crust in the shaded corners of the tread. Above the last visible bend the trail keeps rising toward the peak, the main line up the mountain.
ridge_cabin_switchbacks.examineDetails:
You study the path the way you would a witness who does not know he is talking.

This is not timber staging or plank bridges - only dirt switchbacks cut into the grade and kept open by whoever still has business above the snow line. The steepest pitches show stone kicked through for footing. Drifts lean against the uphill shoulder. Someone has maintained the route recently: a sapling cut back, a slide scraped clear, fresh prints over older ones.

Below you the switchbacks drop away toward the steep mountain trail, the cabin plateau, and the cliff path you left behind. You came up the forward route. The mountain does not encourage hurry up here.

ridge_cabin_exterior.description: The left-hand path opens onto a small snowy plateau where a cabin looks no older than ten years and far better made than most ridge construction. Squared timber walls rise from a stone foundation, corners tight, roof metal and true. A covered porch faces the valley with two rockers and a braided rug. Windows are clean. The chimney is swept. The place is quaint without being precious - the work of someone who knew how to build and did not need to boast about it.
ridge_cabin_exterior.examineDetails:
You walk the perimeter slowly, reading the details the way you read a room.

The logs are dressed and fitted, not the slap-and-chink work of a mining camp. Chinking is neat. The foundation is river stone set in mortar, level on the plateau despite the mountain's tilt all around it. A brass house number - 7 - is nailed beside the door with a carpenter's straightness. The porch boards are recent, still pale where sun has not yet darkened them. Two rockers sit under the eave, cushions faded but dry. A braided rug lies square on the boards as though someone straightened it before leaving.

No name on the mailbox. No horse in a lean-to. No wash on the line. And yet the cabin is not abandoned: window glass unbroken, door hardware oiled, a faint scent of woodsmoke in the stone chimney though no fire burns now. Whoever lives here keeps a small, orderly life above the fog line.

The branch path drops away behind you toward the steep mountain trail below. Through the front door you catch the warm smell of coffee and the deeper hearth-light of the living room beyond the kitchen. This feels like a different kind of appointment - private, domestic, recently chosen.

ridge_cabin_bedroom.description: A modest bedroom tucked under the cabin's roofline, the ceiling following the pitch close enough that a tall man would mind his head. The carved teak bed frame occupies nearly half the room, dressed in a patchwork quilt and plain linens. A nightstand with a lit lamp stands on one side; a dresser on the other. Multiple windows look out over the mountains below, the valley lost in distance and failing light.
ridge_cabin_bedroom.examineDetails:
The bedroom is spare and deliberate, the second room of a house built for one life rather than company.

The bed dominates the space without apology: carved teak headboard and footboard, scrollwork worn smooth where hands have steadied themselves, quilt folded square, pillow kept by habit rather than display. On the nightstand the lamp burns low beside a closed book. Across from it, the dresser holds a mirror, hairbrush, straight razor in a dish, and a pocket watch that has been wound recently.

The windows are the room's real luxury - three of them, clean-latched, framing ridge after ridge dropping away into forest and river far below. No woman's comb or perfume. Whoever sleeps here lives alone and keeps the fact without apology.

The doorway to your left leads back to the living room, and beyond that the kitchen, porch, and mountain trail.

ridge_doctors_practice.description: A small doctor's practice sits on a bench of the mountain road, white clapboard gone chalky with sun and winter, green trim at the windows and porch rail. A painted shingle hangs from the eave: DR. E. HALSTROM - PHYSICIAN & SURGEON. The front steps are swept. A brass knocker is polished. The building looks no older than the century's turn and maintained by someone who still believes patients may arrive on foot.
ridge_doctors_practice.examineDetails:
You read the practice the way you would a man's credentials before you trust him with a wound.

The exterior is modest frontier medicine, not city charity. Clapboard siding, double-hung windows with clean glass, a narrow porch wide enough for a chair and a waiting patient. The shingle is hand-lettered and recently touched up. Beside the door, a slate board lists office hours in chalk that has been rewritten more than once.

Through the front window you catch a glimpse of the waiting room: two hard chairs, a side table with journals, a glass carafe of water, and a cabinet of instruments behind a curtain. No blood on the steps. No abandoned stretcher. Whoever Halstrom is, he keeps a professional face even this far above the valley.

Behind you the steep mountain trail climbs toward the switchbacks and the cabin on the higher bench. The alpine road continues below toward the wider settlement.

cottonwood_meadow.description: The path opens onto grassy hills so green they feel borrowed from another country. An enormous cottonwood rises from the slope ahead, its silver-white bark bright in the sun, its limbs spread wide over rolling turf. The land lifts and swells around the tree like a natural amphitheater. Roughly thirty feet beyond the trunk the plateau ends without warning, dropping away in a sharp edge that turns grass into empty air. Past that brink, mountain after mountain stacks against the sky - blue-gray ridges fading into distance without end.
cottonwood_meadow.examineDetails:
You study the mountains the way a man studies a problem he cannot yet name.

The nearest peaks are timbered to the line, dark spruce giving way to bare rock and late snow in the highest cols. Farther out, the ranges fold into one another, each ridge stealing depth from the next until the horizon is only atmosphere and silhouette. No city smoke. No rail cut. Only wilderness layered to the edge of sight.

The cottonwood stands on the breast of the hill, trunk thick enough that two men could not span it, roots buried deep in the living turf. About thirty feet out from the tree the grassy plateau simply stops. The ground falls away fast - not a gradual slope but a hard break, as though the mountain shrugged off its shoulder and left this shelf behind.

One patch of grass catches your eye before anything else does: a stretch roughly ten feet from the trunk, greener and thicker than the grass around it, vivid enough to feel wrong in all this open light. The grass beneath the tree itself is trampled flat in a rough circle, as though generations of travelers have paused here and never quite agreed to leave.

interactions.label: Rest by the cottonwood and stare at the mountains.
interactions.useDetails: You settle against the cottonwood's trunk, the bark cool through your coat, the grass soft under your boots. The mountains fill your vision - ridge upon ridge, distance made visible. You cannot explain what loosens in your chest as you watch them, only that it does. The air feels easier. Your thoughts grow quiet. For a little while, the ridge stops asking questions you do not know how to answer.

white_baptist_church.description: The white Baptist church sits plain and patient on its rise, paint weathered to the color of old bone. Clapboard siding has gone chalky with sun and winter, corners softened, steeple standing honest against the sky without pretension. The path ends at a low gate. To the right, a small cemetery spreads over the slope in weathered stones and wooden crosses. The building looks worn from the outside - frontier modesty, mountain weather, decades of wind - but not abandoned. The walk is swept. The sanctuary doors stand slightly ajar. No voices carry from within. Whatever service was held here today has already ended.
white_baptist_church.examineDetails:
You study the church the way you would a man who keeps his grief in order.

The exterior tells one story: humble white clapboard, modest steeple, paint gone thin at the eaves, nail heads rusted through in places where the ridge has worked its slow argument with timber. Window frames are sound but not new. The steps are worn hollow at the center where Sunday shoes have passed for years.

And yet the place is tended. Weeds have been cut back from the path. The gate latch closes clean. To the right, the cemetery rows climb the slope in weathered stone and leaning crosses, some plots fresh enough to show recent grief. Through the open sanctuary doors you catch interior wood glowing warm with fresh oil, and colored light from stained glass that has not been allowed to go dark. No congregation remains. Whoever maintains this church does it without display. The building is worn on the outside because mountains wear everything. Inside, someone is still fighting back.

white_baptist_church_interior.description: You step inside and the room opens upward like a held breath released. The sanctuary is far more beautiful than the weathered shell outside promised - soaring timber trusses, a vaulted ceiling cut by craftsmen who understood proportion and prayer in equal measure. Rows of pews run down a wide central aisle toward a plain altar and a raised pulpit. Stained glass throws cobalt, amber, and ruby light across the floor in slow moving patches. Every wooden surface glows with recent linseed oil - pews, rail, beams, window frames - warm and resin-sweet in the nostrils. The building is old. The room is not neglected. But the church is not in service now. The congregation has gone. Hymnals wait in the racks. Brass lamps are trimmed. Dust motes drift in the colored light with no boots to stir them.
white_baptist_church_interior.examineDetails:
You take the measure of the room the way you would a letter written in a hand you almost recognize.

The architecture first. This is not frontier slap carpentry dressed up for Sunday. Hammer-beam trusses lift the ceiling with real engineering and real grace. Posts and braces meet in joints cut to shame a barn rafter. The proportions are deliberate - wide nave, lifted chancel, sightlines that draw the eye to the altar without shouting. Whoever built this church expected God and congregation to meet in the same breath.

The stained glass is the surprise. You expected cracked panes and sun-faded saints. Instead the windows are intact, colors true, lead came straight and honest. Cobalt blues, amber golds, ruby reds - biblical scenes rendered with enough skill to hold a man's attention even if he has forgotten the verses. Light through them falls in clean jeweled shapes across the boards, moving as the day moves.

The woodwork tells the rest. Pews, altar rail, pulpit, ceiling beams - all of it recently coated with linseed oil. You can smell it, warm and resinous, the particular sweetness of fresh maintenance. The surfaces gleam without varnish flash. Whoever did the work knew how to feed old timber instead of burying it under gloss. The floorboards show wear at the aisle center and along the front row, but the room is swept, the hymnals aligned, the lamp chimneys cleaned. A braided rug before the altar is faded but not filthy.

A narrow door at the left of the chancel leads to the vestry. No sound comes from behind it now except the faint scratch of pen on paper.

Outside, the church looks tired. Inside, it looks kept. Not restored to newness - preserved in use. The congregation has gone for today, but someone still shows up with oil, lamp wick, and a broom.

white_baptist_church_vestry.description: You push through the narrow door at the side of the chancel into the vestry - a modest paneled room that smells of ink, lamp oil, and old wool. Vestments hang from a peg beside a shelf crowded with ledgers, hymnals, and church records. A single window throws soft daylight across a worn desk where an aging man in religious garments bends over an open journal, pen moving in careful strokes.
white_baptist_church_vestry.examineDetails:
The vestry is the church's working heart, smaller than the sanctuary but kept with the same stubborn care.

The desk dominates the room: scarred pine, ink stains in the grain, a journal open to a page of tight clerical hand, an iron nib resting in a ceramic tray. Beside it sit a ledger of baptisms and burials, a bottle of ink, and a kerosene lamp trimmed for evening work.

The minister's garments on the peg are plain black, brushed clean, collar pressed with the patience of a man who still believes presentation is part of devotion. His face, when he looks up, is weathered and kind - silver hair, deep lines, eyes that have buried more ridge settlers than he cares to count aloud.

The wood here, like the nave outside, carries the recent sheen of linseed oil. Whoever tends this church does the unglamorous work as faithfully as the visible work.

white_baptist_church_cemetery.description: The cemetery spreads over the church rise in uneven rows, the dead laid out with frontier practicality and mountain weather for company. Headstones lean where frost has heaved the soil. Wooden crosses gray with age stand beside newer granite markers cut plain and honest. Wild grass and alpine flowers push up between the plots. Beyond the low fence, the mountains hold their distance - blue-gray ridges under a quiet sky. The church stands at your back, white paint gone chalky, steeple still pointing upward as though argument with death were worth continuing.
white_baptist_church_cemetery.examineDetails:
You walk the rows the way a man walks a ledger he is not yet ready to sign.

The oldest markers are wood, names weathered to ghosts. A few granite stones carry dates from the seventies and eighties - company men, infants, mothers lost in winter, a prospector who lasted one season and not two. Some plots are tended: weeds cut, pebbles laid flat, a jar of wildflowers gone dry but recent enough to mean someone still comes.

Others have surrendered to the ridge. Sink-holes at the feet of crosses. Names split by frost. A child's stone no larger than a Bible, tilted so far the grass has nearly claimed it.

The cemetery is not large. It is persistent. Highline buries its own because Graysmill is a long descent and the mountain prefers to keep what it takes within sight of the living.

saloon_kitchen.description: The kitchen lies beyond the first left-hand door. Heat, steam, and the honest chaos of a working line. This room has not been fully mapped yet.

saloon_supply_closet.description: The plain pine door gives into a supply closet barely wider than a man's shoulders. A single bare bulb throws hard light on rough-built shelves that occupy the center of the room like an island of unfinished carpentry. Mops and brooms lean along the left wall, their handles dark with use and damp. The shelves are crowded with the unglamorous machinery of keeping a saloon running: glass bottles of cleaning chemicals with faded labels, spare kitchen tins, stacked boxes of nails, a tin of stove polish, coils of twine, and hand tools hung from nails where a carpenter stopped organizing and started surviving. On the front edge of the middle shelf sit a small jar of lamp oil and a hunting knife in a worn leather sheath, placed within easy reach as though someone meant to come back for them.
saloon_supply_closet.examineDetails:
You take stock of the closet in practical terms.

Top shelf: lye soap, stove blacking, unlabeled cleaning bottles sharp with ammonia, and kitchen overflow—enamel cups, a cracked bowl, flour gone hard at the corners.

Middle shelf: nails in rough wooden boxes, a hand plane with a chipped iron, baling wire, and on the front edge a half-full mason jar of lamp oil and a hunting knife in a worn leather sheath. The blade has been honed recently. The handle is polished where a palm has worried it. Neither item belongs to the housekeeping kit.

Lower shelf: rags in a lidded bucket, liniment, spare lamp chimneys in newsprint, and a match tin with a worn striker.

Mops and brooms lean along the left wall; one mop head is still damp. The floor is swept but stained where chemicals have dripped. The air is soap, metal, oil, and old mop water. Only muffled kitchen noise reaches in from the wall.

takeables.name: Lamp Oil
takeables.examineText: A small mason jar, half full of pale golden lamp oil. The glass is wick-yellow at the shoulder where oil has crept up the sides. The cork stopper is stained and fits tight.

takeables.name: Hunting Knife
takeables.examineText: A fixed-blade hunting knife in a dark leather sheath scuffed from carry. The wooden handle is smooth where a palm has worn it. When you ease the blade free, the edge catches the bare bulb's light—recently honed, and kept close at hand.

saloon_back_exit.description: The robust back door gives onto the rear of the building. Cold air threads through the jamb. The service yard beyond has not been fully mapped yet.
